sentences.text: This is my new neighbors.
And I live here just a few weeks, but the people seem to be much more receptive than in the previous place.
I lived there for six years.
And to tell you the truth, very few I even know, not by name, but even by looks.
My next door neighbor, where I went this morning, she's a snowbird-- Italian people from New York.
They're very, very lovely.
And across the way from me, there is an older couple.
They are very, very nice too, but they live here all year round.
Quite of an older-- both of them are over the 80s.
Yeah.
They live here already for 17, 18 years in the same condo.
Well, my name is Eva Hoffman.
My maiden name is Deutsch.
The Deutsch?
D-E-U-T-S-C-H.
And Eva is E-V-A.
And I have no nickname, or a second name.
I was born in Hungary, in Pecs, P-E-C-S, June the 5th, 1928.
Well, I did grow up partially.
I-- as I remember myself, being a small child, we lived in a different town called Szombathely.
It's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about 30, 40 miles from the Austrian border.
It's a small-- smallish town.
And I went there in the first or second grade.
And we moved to Pecs when I was in the second grade, where I was born, and there where I finished my schooling.
Pecs, I would say about 60,000 people, and about 5,000 people of-- 5,000, 6,000 the people Jewish.
Well, there was-- the stores mostly belonged to the Jewish people in the community, and we had a lot of textile and grocery business and [INAUDIBLE] that goes with the small town living.
My grandparents I lived with, they were retired people.
My grandfather was a che-- a chief of the Hungarian railroad, retired.
His name was Frank Pollack.
And this was his second wife, Eugena Pollack.
They had five children between them.
I had-- my mother was the oldest, and she became a nurse after the times being very bad in Hungary's financials goals.
And I had four uncles.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of them was called Emery in English, Emre in Hungarian.
Paul-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Bondy we call him for a nickname, Andor is the full name.
And the youngest one was called Lotze, Laszlo in English.
From all those uncle just remained my uncle, Bondy, who lives still in Hungary, an early age of 92 or so.
In Pecs, the Jews lived very lovely.
They were comfortable to do, should we say.
There was very few who were, should we say, a little bit less established, financially, didn't have good fortune or there were a couple of widow girls who had small children.
One or two used to go like aid days.
This is a old European custom, to feed a meal for a deserving, poor child in a house where food wasn't that scarce.
But beside that, the people were quite comfortable to do.
Well, when I went to the public school-- the Jewish public school-- this was a custom.
Only thing that was a bit difficult, we had to add on car fare for the street car, on account of she lived in the other end of town and I lived in a different end of town.
So this, my grandmother didn't appreciate that she had to add on the car fare to feeding the child.
My parents name was Klara Pollack Deutsch, and Karoly Deutsch, Karl in English.
Well, when my father was originally in the grain business.
But this happened before the years I should be able to remember.
And he lost the business, one way or the other.
Whatever was the reason, I don't really know.
He was a textile man, beside from his childhood [? probably ?] the training, selling like-- selling and buying yard goods.
And this was one of the reasons my mother went to learn being a nurse, with a-- a little child already around to give it to the grandparents to bring up, to be able to earn a better living.
No, we weren't, not orthodox.
And my grandparents didn't keep a kosher kitchen either.
So I would say it's quite on the liberal side of the Jewish religion.
My grandfather used to go to shul in his younger years, but he had a heart condition, so the synagogue was quite a distance from us, so in the older years, he didn't do it.
And I did get a Jewish education, all right.
I started four grades of Jewish school.
Actually, not Jewish.
It's Hebrew.
We learned to read, and we never learned to write.
Just the boys were taught how to write.
And history.
And there was no question about it.
It was compulsory.
We used to go, like, Friday afternoon, like youth services.
But we were very traditional, not orthodox whatsoever.
And the whole town--
Not really.
There wasn't the question, you know, on traveling Shabbat, account of the distances weren't that large as you would think in the United States.
Have to reduce the distances at least to 1/3.
So wherever you wanted to go on the Shabbat, it didn't make that much of a difference.
You mostly walked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But beside that, with electricity and like that, we didn't observe.
I finished four grade of elementary school in the Jewish school, and four grade of [NON-ENGLISH], means-- it's like a high school.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I finished schooling, I was 14 years of age.
Oh, there were already bad times, and went to learn a trade in Budapest.
The joint had, like, a-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] talent school.
No.
What you have a talent to.
The trade-- you have to trace things and draw things and do things, copy things, what they were, you know, like on bulletin boards, to see what you have a talent to.
So they decided that I could be a dressmaker, a milliner, or a flower maker.
You know, like silk flowers and things like that they used on garments.
So my mother did-- insisted that I will become a dressmaker.
I wasn't too happy with that job, but there was no question of any kind of higher education any further in those days.
What was happening in Pecs with your non-Jewish neighbors and with the population?
Well, I left Buda-- for Budapest in 1942.
I haven't got any kind of bad feelings for any of them.
We lived in a villa neighborhood, private homes, individual homes.
One side we had a very lovely neighbor, a youngish couple.
He worked for the waterworks for the city.
And they were very, very pleasant.
How I recall, after the war, my mother picked up a couple of things which one-- my grandmother gave it to them to hide.
And she was-- I corresponded with her for about two years' time, and she wrote to me about the hardships what they went through.
And I haven't got any kind of special feelings.
Nobody done me anything wrong, really.
I had a lot of non-Jewish girlfriends.
We went to school together.
And nobody ever gave me any kind of undesired thought.
Not whatsoever.
Not whatsoever.
That was strictly politics for the grownups.
Children are not supposed to be involved in grownups' conversation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was--
No.
No.
Not whatsoever.
Oh, yeah, we knew that they marched into Poland, which one was, like, over the ocean to us.
That's how far we thought it is.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No.
We felt that we were Hungarians first, and Jewish was just a religion, had nothing to do with one's nationality.
Only trouble is, they started to have inquiries about one's citizenship.
And my grandfather's grandfather was born in Hungary already, and my grandfather was a soldier in Franz Joseph's army.
But he still couldn't prove no citizenship from his birth place.
And all my uncles tried it.
But he still couldn't prove it in paper.
They admitted that my grandfather's-- grandfather was the-- the mayor of the-- of the town.
He was the only per-- person who knew how to read and write.
But they still didn't give him no citizenship paper for it.
Well, I lived in Budapest.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I learned a trade.
And we had a lot of friends we were.
And we had-- this was on a Sunday.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went to the indoor pool, the month of March on [?
Margaretine ?] Island, and they didn't let us in. 10:00 in the morning, they didn't let us in.
They don't--
No.
The Germans came in, but I didn't know what was the reason that we weren't admitted to the pool.
This wasn't just us.
Anybody-- they just didn't let anybody into the pool.
This was the day the Germans came in, the 20th of March, 1944.
After that, we had plenty of other problems.
But this is-- you know, young people are not taking things as seriously as people with a mature mind.
So we were a little bit unhappy.
We were corralled into a ghetto, and--
Well, eventually, [? we ?] all of us went home.
But we couldn't forget until evening what was the reason why they didn't let us in.
Well, the Germans came in and started with the yellow star and the confinement into the ghettos and move together families.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like--
Pardon?
No.
My parents lived in Budapest.
Oh, my parents lived in Budapest.
My mother was learning the trade in Budapest.
No, my parents lived in Budapest.
I just lived with my grandparents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Parents live in Budapest since, I would say, since 1935, '36.
Yeah.
Mama used to come just on vacations.
And I used to go to visit her to Budapest.
Were happy times when I was a little girl.
Well, my mother was a nurse.
She was in the Jewish hospital.
And the Jewish hospital eventually had to get evacuated.
The Germans wanted it for themselves.
The Jewish hospital is actually a great big complex, fenced all around with a port-- portal to come through.
I mean, unauthorized people they just don't let in.
And different building for different diseases.
So we lived on one of the buildings, the children's hospital.
So they made us get out of the hospital altogether.
And they made, in a Jewish school, a hospital, a Jewish hospital.
So we were smuggling out some of the supplies-- injection needles and syringes and towels and sheets and whatever we could.
We wrapped it around ourselves and then the girls stuck in the needles in between the sheets.
And we got undressed about a block away by a-- a Jewish seamstress, which I never knew they were Jewish.
And my parents survived in the hospital.
My father came back from forced labor.
And my mother was over the age what the Germans were more or less interested to use like forced labor.
So they survived in that new Jewish hospital.
And we lived by my uncle, the one which one lives still in Hungary.
He was away in forced labor, and my aunt and her mother and her sister had lived in the apartment.
So they took us in for, like, boarders.
They vacated for us one room, and then another couple came whose-- the menfolks were away, so the three of us, we were in one room.
And then another couple from across the street with two children moved into another room.
So that's how they [INAUDIBLE] actually accumulated the Jewish people under the same roof.
More, the merrier.
Most of the days we spent in the air raid shelter in the basement.
So we had very little freedom of going out or earn a living or anything of that sort.
What the ghetto closed, was--
Not when I was there.
I couldn't go to work account of I worked, like, in the center of the city.
And the air raid usually started around 9:00-- 8:30, 9:00, so--
In the morning, so before you started out, you had to end up in the basement. 5:00 was closing time, should we say.
So that was very difficult.
It took me an hour to get to the city, account of this was a little bit not suburbia, but wasn't in the center of the city.
So I took up a job, after a while-- my mother was very angry-- as a gardeness in a nursery, which one wasn't too far from us, and was pulling weeds in flowerbeds and all that.
So my mother says, no Jewish girl's doing this kind of a job.
I says, listen, this time of age doesn't really matter what kind of a job you are doing.
So I made a few dollar, and the air raid time, I was outdoors.
This was like an open field.
And wasn't too far to travel.
Now, when-- when was this?
What year was this?
When-- when did they establish the ghetto?
Well, the ghetto they established, I would say, the month of May.
Yes.
I was working as a gardener.
It gave me a little bit of training for digging ditches later on, under the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It gave me a little bit of extra training.
[LAUGHS] I was used to the ground like this, eh?
I worked on that job till, like, for the high holidays, sometime like middle of September.
So [INAUDIBLE] for quite a few months.
Well, the food came a little bit simplified.
One of the people who lived with us had a non-Jewish brother-in-law across the street.
He had a vegetable store, so this is-- this Mrs.
Friedman was a very generous soul, so she shared her vegetables and whatever food her sister brought with us.
And Mama bought a little bit of butter on the black market.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bread, that was my specialty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Got acquainted with some young baker boys around the corner in a bakery.
So they used to give me a little bit of bread.
You know, we had it on coupons, the bread.
So they used to give it to me, you know, like, through the back door, a few rolls or-- didn't have to stand in a long line and wait for it.
Too much nourishment, you know, like, we didn't really have.
They gave you, like, 20 ounces of meat or something of that sort for the week.
So sometime we got and sometime we didn't, but the butcher was in the same building.
So very, very little food as buying went.
Was hard to come by account of the air raids, that-- which one didn't help much neither.
Yes.
Yes.
But there was too many of us to get any kind of special acknowledgement or help from it.
Even I had an extra acquaintance there.
My rabbi from Pecs, he became one of the three of the Judenrat.
He expired, poor thing, just-- luckily, maybe, should we say, that-- in the wartime he died.
A young man.
Zolta Wallenstein.
Yes.
Yes.
He became one of the-- the three rabbis from the Judenrat.
They did help people.
But people were very busy trying to find lost sons, husbands, which one were taken away for forced labor to [INAUDIBLE].
So this was the-- the biggest job what they were trying to achieve.
And then there was people, you know, they have no jobs, no means.
They're being dislocated account of the ghetto.
And they were-- they did feed the people to certain degree.
But that's all what it was to it.
Really they couldn't do much.
The Germans were asking for extremely amo-- big amounts of bribes for any kind of an action they could come with up.
Not to my knowledge.
Not to my knowledge.
I was a youngster of 16 years.
Maybe there was.
Maybe there wasn't.
But you see, that-- the young people are very busy for themselves.
Self-preservation, maybe you would call it?
And there was nobody, really, who should be giving advice on the things, you know, that could organize.
I had a very good friend.
He was a cleaner for the Gestapo, one of the fancy hotels on the hill.
The rich Jews, which were more, should we say, above the age who were took for forced labor or some physical inability, they did do a lot of torture, the Germans.
They wanted to know about monies and jewels and secrets and things which [INAUDIBLE] people have no access to, after my own knowledge.
There were quite a few big manufacturers in Budapest of Jewish faith.

Which one had metal manufacturing and machinery manufacturing, they wanted to have information out of them, if they have any kind of connection to the Americans, the English or-- tremendous amount of torture was going on out there.
Not really that I should say that I had seen anything.
Whatever I heard from the young man, but he couldn't name no names.
Account of he cleaned up, excuse me, whatever that came out afterwards, you know, like, they tortured them, the blood or whatever else.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was his job.
I don't even know if--
He was just telling me what he was doing the day before.
Well, we lived in the same building, so we didn't go out of our ways to meet or anything.
He was in the same ghetto building as I was.
So air raid time, have to entertain with something besides playing cards.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, no special-- anything that I could recall.
A couple of name-calling here and there, but I was not that long around that I should have any kind of a special [INAUDIBLE].
His name was [?
Gluk. ?] But [?
Gluk ?] what I don't remember already what was his name.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a good friend of mine.
